item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image belongs to Lord Jim, under CC BY 2.0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, it's formerly beneficial graffiti that became vengeful after it's maker died?

Neat!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, I thought that the Keter file was just a cover, and the effect completely disappeared when he died.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dont really like the first part, but the expunged bit prevents me from downvoting

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The collapsible reads "IDENTIFY VERIFIED". I think it’s supposed to be identity?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't forget SCP-274.

item.title: Re: +50
item.content:encoded: Thanks!

item.title: Re: +50
item.content:encoded: Congratulations! It is a landmark. I remember when 1440 first reached 50, there's no feeling quite like it. And as it rose, I'm sure yours will too.
Finally, my first article to reach +50.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-Takes a bow-

Yes, it is three, Predatory Street Art, Watch for the White Bird, and this one.

What's more amusing is that none of them are related to AWCY.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You'd think three (might be two, but I think it's three) graffiti-related keters would be too much, but this one is very well done.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The depopulation is the containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of this tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Depopulation by economic depression… it's okay, I guess, but I can't get worked up over it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) I certainly took the meaning, I've just never heard gas pipes called "veins" before. Might be a regional/cultural thing. Like "soda" versus "pop".

2) I don't know about "brilliant", but thank you all the same. You could have the container city be Detroit or some similar real world economic disaster area. But think carefully on that, tieing to real world problems can backfire badly.

3) Those are certainly the details that clued me in, so they do the job. I was just thinking on the readers you may have lost because they didn't pick up on them. Either way really, it's your call.

4) Seems to be a matter of head canon. I've always thought that what we are reading on the site is general records. But I can see how one might imagine otherwise, particularly in light of some of the -001 proposals and the implications of their formatting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to say this article really clicked for me. I attended Temple university in Philadelphia and, while the campus is safe, I can't say the same for the surrounding areas. While not all urban graffiti is by it's nature bad, sometimes just walking through heavily graffitied areas gives a sense of unease or even frustration about the way thing are in the area. I thought this skip encapsulated that very well. Up vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1: Yes, though I think "vein" is a usable (albeit less clinical) term.

2: This is actually pretty brilliant. Thank you, I will consider this for the moment.

3: I think this is okay as is; the triple digit designation, the 1980s dates, and the unusually started description are designed to give the "old school foundation" vibe.

4: Well "general records" is supposed to imply that it's not fully destroyed, just not available for most security levels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, that's even better! And not just positive, but directed and contained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From the containment "…a gas vein explosion…", is that supposed to be a gas main?

The containment procedures don't seem to be doing much containing. It's just that evacuating the area gaurantees that it will jump and need to be found all over again. I think that the Foundation would be trying to design something that leaves it pinned down, and would be willing to sacrifice the economy of a city to do it. For example, follow the current procedures until it lands in a city that meets the criteria but is relatively small and, more importantly, does not sprawl. The the Foundation either takes steps to empty the city, or let's the natural exodus take its course, while at the same time establishing a permanent baracks. A city rusts to nothing, but as long as there is a single person still living there, and the surrounding area doesn't get too densely built up, the angel of poverty is contained.

At part two, I was confused for a bit until I realized that the collapsible was the first version of documentation. It might flow better if you made that clearer earlier.

"Expunged" implies the data was erased totally, which does not jibe with us (ostensibly users on the Foundation network) sitting here reading it. Aside from which, what is the in-universe justification for double-secreting the original documentation? This isn't one where the powers that be have any reason to sweep it under the rug, and the way the anomaly has changed is useful data that could help a researcher figure it out.

Quibbles aside, I quite like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just listing a big city as an example.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For what it's worth, Detroit was exactly where my head went while reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think that the second paragraph of the description might need some extra detail. I'm interested in the effect, but it was so brief that I was left wanting to know more on exactly how this functioned. And not in the "Oh man this is good but I want to know more" kind of way, more in the "really this is all he's gonna tell me" kind of way. I like the article, but it does feel like the actual effect is described very lightly.

Other than that though, very nice job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get what you're going for, and you did a pretty good job executing it, but it's just not doing anything for me.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm with Azzle on this. I want to like it. I really do. But despite the mountain of extra material (a whole second SCiP!), it still feels somehow incomplete.

But I do like the story. It's interesting, and yet I also feel it lacks a certain resolution.

No vote. And I almost feel bad about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, aparently I wasn't the only one who noticed the self-insert (and I'm neutral about it by the way). Now, it's really good and shows what happen when the Foundation screws up something aparently harmless. And wel, as Pig_Catapult said, Termination is a ephemism for an intentional kill.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not New York, which, despite its low points, has never descended into the total economic collapse implied by this. It killed Detroit. Or Baltimore. Or any number of dying/dead Rust Belt cities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh crap; fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not contained. It's not like they set out to see just how catastrophic it could get. This thing went crazy after the son's death, and started torpedoing the economy of, say, New York. It wasn't for a while that the Foundation figured out how to make it stop, so they got to see the effects in full.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not really sure about this one.. I'm on the fence.

Also, at the end of the log, it still says, "<Begin Log, 4:47 PM, ██/██/198█>" believe it should say, <End Log, 4:47 PM, ██/██/198█>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno about this one. I really liked where it was headed, but the ending felt very flat and… off, to me, I guess. I dunno, it didn't feel all the way resolved, really. However, I enjoyed the rest of the article, so I'm going to do something I usually don't do and neutral vote until I can get a clearer opinion on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What a coincidence, my avatar's father also died in one of my articles but that was due to me not paying attention to what dates I was putting down in the incident log but oh well

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has a believability problem for me. IMO there's no way the Foundation would have been able to discern the effects of 1824 without causing a security breach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'l make the necessary adjustments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think they flipped. I think the 'original' was the only thing keeping the effect positive. And because it died, there's no one to, well, forgive us for this stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're using "terminate" wrong at the end. "Terminate" is a specific euphemism the Foundation uses for when they intentionally execute someone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an interesting one.

The story behind it is nice, and it's neat to see a Keter that isn't "kill everything."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the origin log, and how his effects flipped when he died.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The author avatar thing is just fine.

You fixed up some of the stuff we discussed last night, and it's a solid enough effort for me to upvote. I sort of preferred the increased ambiguity about the nature of the SCP, but this works well enough. I feel this could be better with a bit more work, but this is basically fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that does happen a lot, :) but it's cool. I haven't stated Anborough's given name in any of my articles (but this is the first article where he's mentioned at all), though a seven letter name is blacked out on my personnel page. It's "Charles".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno, somehow I got it into my head that your name was David because of David Attenborough. I'm sure you get it a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait a minute, where did the name "David" come from?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno if I would discourage a "self-insert" like this. When I saw the doctor's name was "Brian" instead of "David" I was a bit confused until I realized David is Brian's son, which is a level of depth you don't normally see in self-insert type dealies. If anything, I would love to see continuing mentions by the now deceased Brian Anborough or the newly hired David Anborough in other SCPs. If they were to occasionally refer to each other I think that would make for a very touching continuous canon, like what happened with Eric.

But that's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually really like author avatar character types when done well, especially when the author uses them to make their own little universe. Like how Azzleflux uses Agent Fredericks, and I use Rosen/Dr. Boyd. The article is also really cool, and does a good job with its twist collapsible. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It took a minute for this to click, but I quite like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The self insert isnt a big deal in my opinion. Its just a name drop. He isnt going out saving the world or any other badass thing.

As for the article itself I don't get it (possibly because I have a killer headache atm) but the feeling is just enough for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. Fair enough.

2. As you wish; I wasn't sure whether the secondary file counted towards the tags.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Still a self-insert by proxy, in my opinion.

Please let me do the tags next time.

Edit: Self-inserts aren't necessarily bad. It's just still a self-insert because, well, it's your name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's actually my avatar's father who died back in the eighties.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The self-insert's a bit iffy, but the rest of it is excellent
